edges.description: The leak-current susceptibility enables paradoxical sustained depolarization in low extracellular potassium.
Loss of skeletal-muscle excitability during attacks reduces force generation and produces focal or generalized flaccid weakness.
Progressive myopathic change produces fixed weakness between attacks.

nodes.description: Acetazolamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor used for attack prevention in selected patients, with response varying by genotype and lower response reported in SCN4A-related disease.

evidence.reference_title: Acetazolamide efficacy in hypokalemic periodic paralysis and the predictive role of genotype.
evidence.snippet: Only 46% of the total patient cohort (34 of 74) reported benefit from acetazolamide.

evidence.reference_title: Targeted Therapies for Skeletal Muscle Ion Channelopathies: Systematic Review and Steps Towards Precision Medicine.
evidence.snippet: hypokalemic PP patients carrying sodium channel mutations may have fewer benefits from CAI compared to those carrying calcium channel mutations.

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: HypoPP is associated with mutations in calcium channel (CACNA1S; 60% of kindreds) and sodium channel (SCN4A; 20% of kindreds) genes.

evidence.reference_title: A novel CACNA1S gene variant in a child with hypokalemic periodic paralysis: a case report and literature review.
evidence.snippet: Pathogenic variants of CACNA1S can cause hypokalemic periodic paralysis (HypoPP), malignant hyperthermia susceptibility, and congenital myopathy.

nodes.description: Dichlorphenamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor with randomized trial evidence for reducing episodic weakness in primary periodic paralyses, including hypokalemic periodic paralysis.

evidence.reference_title: Randomized trials of dichlorphenamide in the periodic paralyses. Working Group on Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: We performed two multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover trials, one involving 42 subjects with hypokalemic periodic paralysis (HypoPP)

evidence.reference_title: Randomized trials of dichlorphenamide in the periodic paralyses. Working Group on Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: We conclude that DCP is effective in the prevention of episodic weakness in both HypoPP and PSPP.

nodes.description: Recurrent focal or generalized flaccid skeletal-muscle weakness or paralysis that lasts hours to days and may be precipitated by diet, alcohol, stress, or rest after strenuous exercise.

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: HypoPP is characterized by focal or generalized paralytic episodes of skeletal muscle, which can last hours to days and are associated with concomitant hypokalemia (<2.5 mEq/L).

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: Positive triggers (high carbohydrate rich meal, rest after exercise, stress)

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: The first attack most commonly occurs in the first 2 decades of life, and rarely after 30 years of age.

nodes.description: CACNA1S or SCN4A variants in skeletal-muscle ion channels can generate an anomalous leakage current that is active at resting potential, making muscle fibers susceptible to paradoxical depolarization in low extracellular potassium.

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: HypoPP is associated with mutations in calcium channel (CACNA1S; 60% of kindreds) and sodium channel (SCN4A; 20% of kindreds) genes.

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: homologous gene defects of either channel cause an anomalous leakage current, which is active at the resting potential and produces susceptibility to paradoxical depolarization of the fiber and inexcitability in the setting of low extracellular K+ (2.5 to 3.5 Meq/L).

nodes.description: Paralytic episodes are associated with reduced serum potassium; documented low potassium during attacks supports diagnosis.

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: Two or more attacks of muscle weakness with documented serum K <3.5 mEq/L

evidence.reference_title: Case report: SCN4A p.R1135H gene variant in combination with thyrotoxicosis causing hypokalemic periodic paralysis.
evidence.snippet: with a serum potassium level as low as 1.59 mmol/L.

nodes.description: Some individuals develop fixed, progressive weakness between attacks, often involving proximal lower-limb muscles and reflecting progressive myopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: A variable myopathy develops in many affected individuals and may result in a progressive muscle weakness predominantly in proximal muscle groups of the lower limbs.

evidence.reference_title: Hypokalemic periodic paralysis: a 3-year follow-up study.
evidence.snippet: The study demonstrates that HypoPP can be a progressive myopathy in both patients with and without attacks of paralysis.

nodes.description: Oral potassium chloride is used to treat acute hypokalemic attacks when clinically appropriate; serum potassium and ECG monitoring are important to avoid overshoot or arrhythmia.

evidence.reference_title: Practical aspects in the management of hypokalemic periodic paralysis.
evidence.snippet: Potassium chloride is the favored potassium salt given at 0.5-1.0 mEq/kg for acute attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: Monitor ECG and potassium levels.

nodes.description: Potassium-sparing diuretics such as triamterene, spironolactone, or eplerenone are chronic preventive options for reducing HypoPP attack frequency and severity, with potassium monitoring.

evidence.reference_title: Practical aspects in the management of hypokalemic periodic paralysis.
evidence.snippet: Chronically, acetazolamide, dichlorphenamide, or potassium-sparing diuretics decrease attack frequency and severity

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: Recommended doses are triamterene 50–150 mg/day, spironolactone 25–100 mg/day or eplerenone 50–100 mg daily.

nodes.description: A subset of affected individuals develop fixed or progressive myopathy with permanent weakness and increasing fatty replacement of skeletal muscle, which can progress even in people without frequent paralytic attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Hypokalemic periodic paralysis: a 3-year follow-up study.
evidence.snippet: Muscle strength declined in 11 patients during follow-up.

evidence.reference_title: Hypokalemic periodic paralysis: a 3-year follow-up study.
evidence.snippet: Fat replacement of muscles increased in 27 patients during follow-up.

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: HypoPP is associated with mutations in calcium channel (CACNA1S; 60% of kindreds) and sodium channel (SCN4A; 20% of kindreds) genes.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: SCN4A p.R1135H gene variant in combination with thyrotoxicosis causing hypokalemic periodic paralysis.
evidence.snippet: genetic testing indicated a missense variant, NM_000334.4 (SCN4A):c.3404G>A (p.R1135H).

nodes.description: During attacks, sarcolemmal depolarization inactivates sodium channels and reduces skeletal-muscle fiber excitability, causing acute paresis.

evidence.reference_title: Review of the Diagnosis and Treatment of Periodic Paralysis.
evidence.snippet: In all forms of PP, ictal paresis is caused by depolarization of the muscle sarcolemma, which in turn causes sodium channel inactivation and reduced fiber excitability.

nodes.description: Dietary and behavioral counseling targets common triggers such as high-carbohydrate meals, high salt intake, alcohol, stress, and rest after strenuous exercise.

evidence.reference_title: Practical aspects in the management of hypokalemic periodic paralysis.
evidence.snippet: Avoidance of or potassium prophylaxis for common triggers, such as rest after exercise, high carbohydrate meals, and sodium, can prevent attacks.

evidence.reference_title: A novel CACNA1S gene variant in a child with hypokalemic periodic paralysis: a case report and literature review.
evidence.snippet: The attacks of the patient are prevented by lifestyle changes and nutritional counseling.
